retrieved_docs.title: Incomplete Miscarriage -- Treatment / Management -- Expectant and Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: If medical management is selected, ACOG recommends vaginal misoprostol 800 mcg once initially, with a potential second dose between 3 hours and 7 days later. [4] The World Health Organization prefers misoprostol regimens of 400 mcg sublingually once or 600 mcg orally only once for pregnancies <14 weeks of gestation. [7] One large study of women with incomplete abortion showed that the rate of complete abortion within 14 to 28 days of initial treatment with misoprostol without surgical intervention was 96.2%. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Abortion Complications -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: For first-trimester medical abortion, the most common regimen is oral mifepristone followed 24 hours later by misoprostol, which may be administered vaginally, sublingually, or buccally. [2] Mifepristone is a progesterone receptor blocker that enhances the efficacy of misoprostol, a synthetic prostaglandin that induces uterine contractions, softens the cervix, and facilitates the expulsion of the pregnancy within 8 hours of administration. [2] [4] Analgesics and antiemetics, such as ibuprofen and ondansetron, are often administered prophylactically in anticipation of the predictably strong cramping, moderate to heavy bleeding, and nausea that occur during 1 to 2 hours in which the pregnancy is expelled. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Issues of Concern -- Nausea and vomiting
retrieved_docs.content: Nausea and vomiting occur in approximately half of pregnant patients, most commonly between 4 and 12 weeks gestation. Physiologic causes are multifactorial, including rapidly increasing levels of pregnancy-related hormones such as β-human chorionic gonadotropin, estrogen, progesterone, placental growth hormone, and leptin. In patients with severe nausea and vomiting or symptoms persisting beyond the first trimester, other obstetric (eg, molar pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum) and nonobstetric (eg, appendicitis and thyroid disease) etiologies should be considered. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of mifepristone and misoprostol is FDA-approved for medical abortion, although state regulations may vary. [6] Women seeking medical management for first-trimester miscarriage should be informed by obstetricians that misoprostol monotherapy can be used off-label. Delays in the medical management of elective abortion may lead to higher healthcare costs, as women contemplate the cost and potential concerns about the "off-label" use of treatments. Such delays also increase the risk of complications, emergency department visits, and the need for surgical evacuation. [1] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Prenatal Management in the Third Trimester
retrieved_docs.content: If emergent maternal-fetal-associated complications arise, patients may require induction of labor or imminent delivery depending on the circumstance and severity. [21] [22] Additionally, as patients reach or approach their expected date of delivery, various issues may need to be discussed with shared decision-making, such as labor induction, post-term pregnancy, and delivery, as well as continued patient education regarding peripartum and postpartum topics, including labor signs, breastfeeding, and contraception. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Pregnancy Loss (Spontaneous Abortion) -- Treatment / Management -- Expectant and Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Medications: Misoprostol is the primary drug used to manage early pregnancy loss. Although numerous different misoprostol regimens have been used, the regimen currently recommended by ACOG consists of misoprostol 800 mcg vaginally once daily as a single dose; if the expulsion of POC does not occur, the dose may be repeated once, any time from 3 hours to 7 days after the first dose. Premedication with mifepristone 200 mg orally once daily as a single dose, 24 hours before the first dose of misoprostol, results in a higher success rate than misoprostol alone and is recommended by most experts, if available. [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Induction of Labor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine fetal demise

retrieved_docs.title: Early Pregnancy Loss (Spontaneous Abortion) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Medical management of early pregnancy loss is best achieved with a combination of misoprostol and mifepristone, though it can also be managed with misoprostol alone if mifepristone is unavailable. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Abortion -- Complications -- Complications of Medication Abortion and Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management involves ibuprofen 800 mg. Uterine aspiration procedure. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Induction of Labor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Abruptio placentae

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol use in pregnant women has been associated with premature birth, congenital abnormalities, and abortion. A risk of uterine rupture exists with misoprostol, particularly when used for labor induction beyond 8 weeks of gestation. This risk is heightened by additional factors such as a history of cesarean delivery. Uterine rupture is rare during first-trimester medical abortion with misoprostol. However, as with any uterine rupture, there is a potential risk of subsequent uterine infection. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Abortion -- Technique or Treatment -- Mifepristone-misoprostol protocol
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol: This drug can be administered using one of the following routes: Buccal: A dosage of 4 misoprostol tablets, 200 mcg each, is placed between the gum and cheek for 30 minutes and swallowed thereafter, 24 to 48 hours after oral mifepristone administration. This is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO). Vaginal: A dosage of 4 misoprostol tablets, 200 mcg each, may be inserted into the vagina 6 to 48 hours after oral mifepristone administration. This is recommended by the WHO. Sublingual: A dosage of 2 to 4 misoprostol tablets, 200 mcg each, is placed under the tongue for 30 minutes. This is recommended by the WHO.

retrieved_docs.title: Incomplete Miscarriage -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Following treatment, patients should be counseled to expect to continue bleeding for 1 to 2 weeks, sometimes longer, particularly with expectant management. Clinicians should also provide patients precautions, instructing them to return for evaluation if bleeding is heavy enough to soak through 1 to 2 pads per hour for 2 consecutive hours, if the pain is uncontrolled by over-the-counter analgesics, or if a fever develops. Patients should also be scheduled for follow-up to confirm the complete expulsion of POC from the uterus. Typically this is done with a pelvic ultrasound; however, a decrease in β-hCG levels by 50% after 2  days or 87% by 7 days may also be used if ultrasound is unavailable. [2] Patients can also be reassured that uncomplicated pregnancy loss does not impact future fertility. Additionally, ovulation often resumes within the first cycle after the expulsion of fetal tissues, so patients should be offered contraception if they do not desire another pregnancy right away. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Abortion Complications -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Heavy or prolonged uterine bleeding after first-trimester medical abortion is typically the result of incomplete abortion and can be managed in several ways. Expectant management with close follow-up may be appropriate for clinically stable patients who wish to avoid further intervention. [2] [4] Alternatively, a repeat dose of misoprostol (600 or 800 mcg through the buccal or sublingual route) may be appropriate, [2] [4] keeping in mind that this leads to a transient increase in bleeding and cramps. Uterine aspiration by a trained clinician is the definitive treatment for incomplete abortion and is the best choice for patients with significant blood loss. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Abortion -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Medication abortion, also known as self-managed abortion, offers patients greater control and privacy. Nationally and internationally recognized regimens include the use of mifepristone followed by misoprostol. When mifepristone is unavailable, misoprostol alone is an acceptable alternative. [4] Medication abortion after the first trimester can also be performed safely and effectively by trained clinicians in appropriately equipped settings to support the patient. Medication abortion may be preferred over procedural management in the following cases: Uterine fibroids that significantly distort the uterus or cervical canal Congenital uterine anomalies Introital scarring related to infibulation [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2755
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), indicating that women who undergo an elective abortion may have an increased risk of developing cervical weakness that can lead to second‑trimester loss or preterm delivery. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians monitor cervical integrity in patients with a history of elective termination and consider preventive strategies, such as cervical cerclage or closer surveillance, to improve pregnancy outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1139
retrieved_docs.content: Induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of the first trimester, surgical treatment of septic abortion, and therapeutic termination of pregnancy by insertion of laminaria are all specific procedures that fall under the broader category of abortion (إجهاض). Each of these interventions represents a narrower or directly related form of abortion, illustrating the various clinical approaches used to manage pregnancy loss or intentional termination across different gestational ages and clinical circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: إنهاء الحمل - اختياري, which denotes an elective termination of pregnancy, is clinically associated with تهديد بالإجهاض, a condition described as threatened abortion. This clinical association indicates that the two obstetric entities share overlapping concerns, and that the presence of a threatened abortion may influence considerations regarding elective pregnancy termination in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of the uterus after an abortion, aspiration curettage of the uterus for termination of pregnancy, surgical treatment of a missed abortion in the second trimester, surgical treatment of a missed miscarriage in the first trimester, surgical treatment of a missed miscarriage in the third trimester, and surgical treatment of a septic abortion are all clinical procedures that are directly related to the medical condition of abortion (إجهاض). These interventions represent the range of therapeutic approaches used to manage different types and timings of abortion, including elective termination, missed abortions or miscarriages at various gestational ages, and infections complicating abortion. Recognizing that each of these procedures falls under the broader category of abortion helps clinicians categorize care pathways, select appropriate management strategies, and ensure comprehensive patient care for individuals experiencing any form of pregnancy loss.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2162
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus after abortion, induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy, surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of the third trimester, and therapeutic termination of pregnancy by insertion of laminaria are all medical procedures that fall under the broader category of إجهاض (abortion). The first, third, and fourth procedures are directly related to abortion, while the second and fifth are described as having a narrower relationship, indicating they represent more specific or specialized forms of induced termination. Together, these interventions illustrate the range of surgical and therapeutic techniques used to manage both elective and missed abortions across different stages of pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2409
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus for termination of pregnancy, hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, and surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of the third trimester are all medical procedures that are directly related to إجهاض (abortion). Within this broader category, hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy as well as surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester represent narrower, more specific forms of abortion care. The procedures for missed abortion in the second trimester and missed miscarriage in the third trimester are also explicitly linked to إجهاض, highlighting their role in managing pregnancy loss at different stages.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 871
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus after abortion, induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed abortion of second trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of third trimester, and surgical treatment of septic abortion are all procedures that are linked to the clinical entity of abortion (إجهاض). Among them, induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy is identified as a narrower, more specific form of abortion, while the other procedures are described as having a general relationship with abortion. These connections highlight that each of these surgical or medical interventions is performed in the context of managing or terminating a pregnancy, encompassing a range of clinical scenarios from routine curettage after an abortion to the treatment of missed or septic abortions across different trimesters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1692
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), indicating that this surgical technique is used to support a weakened cervix and prevent premature dilation in patients with this condition. In addition, optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, reflecting that pregnancy termination may be considered as a management option in severe cases where the insufficiency poses significant risk to maternal or fetal health. Both interventions are relevant clinical considerations in the assessment and treatment of cervical insufficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are both hormonal agents that may help prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In contrast, a history of CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY is clinically associated with an increased likelihood of إجهاض تلقائي, indicating that prior fetal structural anomalies can be a risk factor for subsequent spontaneous loss. Understanding that 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone can be used prophylactically, while recognizing the heightened risk linked to previous congenital malformations, is important for optimizing patient counseling and management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2339
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics excision is a broad surgical category in obstetric care that encompasses several specific procedures. It includes the surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, as well as the surgical treatment of missed abortion of the second trimester, missed miscarriage of the first trimester, and missed miscarriage of the third trimester. In addition, Obstetrics excision also comprises termination of an ectopic pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل خارج الرحم) and lower‑segment cesarean delivery (ولادة قيصرية في الجزء السفلي). Together, these child procedures illustrate the range of obstetric conditions that can be managed through excisional surgical techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus after abortion, aspiration curettage of uterus for termination of pregnancy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of first trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of third trimester, and therapeutic termination of pregnancy by insertion of laminaria are all medical procedures that fall under the broader category of إجهاض (abortion). The procedures described as having a “narrower relationship” – surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of first trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of third trimester, and therapeutic termination of pregnancy by insertion of laminaria – represent specific subtypes within the general concept of abortion. These interventions are employed in the clinical management of both spontaneous miscarriage and elective termination of pregnancy, providing clinicians with a range of surgical and therapeutic options tailored to the gestational age and clinical circumstances of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of uterus after abortion, aspiration curettage of uterus for termination of pregnancy, surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of first trimester, surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of third trimester, and surgical treatment of septic abortion are all medical procedures that are directly related to abortion (إجهاض). These procedures represent various surgical approaches to manage abortion and its complications, ranging from routine curettage after a completed abortion or for elective termination, to the surgical management of missed miscarriages in early and late pregnancy, and to the treatment of septic abortion. The surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester is a more specific subset within the broader category of abortion care. Collectively, these interventions illustrate the spectrum of surgical care associated with abortion and its related obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 913
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage of the uterus after an abortion, hysterotomy‑based termination of pregnancy, induced termination following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy, and the various forms of surgical management of miscarriage—including procedures for any trimester, missed abortion in the second trimester, and septic abortion—are all classified under the broader category of abortion (إجهاض). The latter three procedures represent more specific, narrower subtypes of abortion care, each tailored to particular clinical scenarios such as gestational age, type of fetal loss, or presence of infection. Collectively, these surgical interventions constitute the spectrum of operative treatment options for terminating or managing pregnancy loss, highlighting the importance of selecting the appropriate technique based on the underlying condition and timing of the abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم) is a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain closure during pregnancy, often leading to second‑trimester loss. Recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent or underlying factor linked to cervical insufficiency, underscoring the need to assess cervical competence in patients with habitual abortion. The cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency as a therapeutic intervention that reinforces the cervix to prevent premature dilation and improve pregnancy outcomes. Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, indicating that cervical competence may influence the decision‑making and management of planned pregnancy termination. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose, counsel, and manage women at risk for cervical insufficiency and its related obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2342
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics excision is a category of surgical interventions used to terminate or manage pregnancies under various clinical circumstances. Procedures classified under obstetrics excision include Hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy and Induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysterotomy. The same category also comprises the Surgical treatment of missed miscarriage of first trimester and the Surgical treatment of missed abortion of second trimester. In addition, obstetrics excision encompasses إنهاء الحمل خارج الرحم (termination of an ectopic pregnancy) and ولادة قيصرية في الجزء السفلي (lower‑segment cesarean delivery). These related procedures share the common goal of safely removing fetal tissue or delivering the fetus when continuation of pregnancy is not viable or when maternal health requires intervention. Understanding the relationships among these techniques helps clinicians select the appropriate surgical approach based on gestational age, location of the pregnancy, and specific clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics excision is a surgical category that includes a range of procedures used to manage pregnancy loss and delivery. Within this category, “Hysterotomy and termination of pregnancy” and “Induced termination of pregnancy following intra‑amniotic injection with hysteresis” are specific techniques for ending a pregnancy surgically. Obstetrics excision also encompasses the “Surgical treatment of miscarriage of any trimester,” providing a universal approach to miscarriage care, as well as the “Surgical treatment of missed abortion of second trimester,” which addresses missed abortions specifically in the second trimester. In addition, the category includes “إنهاء الحمل خارج الرحم” (termination of an ectopic pregnancy) and “ولادة قيصرية في الجزء السفلي” (lower‑segment cesarean delivery), extending its scope to the surgical management of ectopic pregnancies and the performance of cesarean sections. Together, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive role of obstetric excision techniques in safely terminating pregnancies, treating various forms of miscarriage, and delivering infants when vaginal birth is not feasible.
